title: Chapter 8: The Frequency
content: Midnight comes and goes like a guest who won't leave.

I'm on the floor. The studio floor — old pine boards warped by a century of New York summers, gaps between the planks wide enough to catch a fingernail. I lie on my back with my arms at my sides and stare at the ceiling, at the water stain that's shaped like a lung, at the fixture where a bulb died three days ago and I still haven't changed it. The dark doesn't bother me. The dark is honest. It's the hum that's the problem.
It's always the hum now.

A low vibration that starts somewhere below my sternum and radiates outward — through my ribs, along my spine, into the marrow of my bones. Like a bass note played in a room I can't find. Like a tuning fork pressed to the inside of my skeleton. I press my palms flat against the floorboards and feel it there too, traveling through the wood, through the joists, through the plaster and lath and brick of this old garment-district building, through the bedrock beneath Manhattan, through the century.
The hum doesn't come from outside. I know that. I've known that since I was fourteen and woke up in a body that wasn't the one I went to sleep in, humming like a bell that had just been struck.
Nine years.

The longest I've ever gone without shifting is nine years. I checked my journals — the leather ones stacked in the closet, the ones with the careful notations, dates, durations, what I was wearing, what I was eating, who I was kissing. Nine years and three days, between my second shift and my third. I was twenty-three then. I'm thirty-two now.
Nine years.

I lie on the floor and do the math and feel the hum climbing through the floorboards into my palms and I think: the silence before a storm doesn't mean the storm has passed. It means you're in the eye.
My hands are trembling. I press them harder into the wood.

The knock comes at twenty past one. I know it's Suki before I even get up because she knocks in triplet — knock-knock-knock, pause, knock — like a heartbeat with a stutter. It's her signature. She started doing it in design school because she said it was "brand consistency." Suki Tanaka: a woman who treats her own personality as a design project and executes it with terrifying precision.

I open the door and she's standing in the hallway with a bottle of Woodford Reserve in one hand and her phone in the other, gum snapping between her teeth. She's wearing her work clothes still — the oversized blazer, the wide-leg trousers, the architectural glasses she doesn't need but refuses to abandon because they're "part of the vocabulary." Her black hair is falling out of its clip. She's been running.
"Get in here," I say.
She walks past me and sets the bottle on the cutting table, nudging aside a bolt of charcoal wool. Then she turns and looks at me with those dark, steady eyes that miss absolutely nothing.
"You look like shit," she says.
"Thank you."
"I mean it. When's the last time you slept?"
I consider lying. Suki can always tell when I'm lying, so I don't bother. "Thursday."
"Orlando. It's Sunday."
"I'm aware."

She pulls the cork from the bourbon with her teeth — a habit I've stopped commenting on — and pours two fingers into a juice glass she finds on the windowsill. She rinses it first, at least. Then she pours her own and holds mine out to me.
"Drink," she says. "Then I'm going to tell you something, and you're going to need it."

I take the glass. The bourbon hits my tongue and slides down my throat and I wait for the burn — the warm, amber sweetness that Suki swears by, the slow heat that's supposed to unknot something in your chest. It barely registers. Like drinking water that's been told a story about fire.

Suki watches me swallow. Watches my face for the flinch that doesn't come. Her gum slows. That's her thinking rhythm — snap, snap, snap when she's processing something, slow molasses chew when she's deciding whether to say it.
"I heard something today," she says.
"The industry grapevine?"

"Is there any other?" She settles onto the stool by my dress form, crossing her legs at the ankle. "Rowan Ashworth has canceled consultations with three designers this week. Three. Margot Ellison. Ji-yeon Park. That kid from Central Saint Martins who did the deconstructed tailoring thing at London Fashion Week."
I set the glass down. My hand is steady. I'm proud of that. "So?"

"So he's only commissioning you." She lets it land. "You. Singular. One designer. Rowan Ashworth — the man who could have anyone in the world wrap him in fabric — is going exclusively through your studio."
The hum surges. I feel it in my teeth.
"Babe," Suki says, pouring another two fingers into my glass without asking, "this is either the break of your life or the beginning of a very specific kind of trouble."
I pick up the glass. "Why not both?"

She doesn't laugh. She watches me drink, and the burn is a little more real this time — a lick of warmth at the back of my throat, a small fire that reminds me I still have a body, that I'm still anchored here, that I haven't vibrated apart yet.
Suki chews slowly. Her thinking face.
"Why just you?" she asks.
"He liked the suit."
"The suit isn't finished."

"He liked the fitting." I take another sip. The words are easier with the bourbon lubricating them. "The way the fabric draped. The shoulder line. The way the canvas and the wool interact when he moves."
"You're talking about a suit like it's poetry."
"Good tailoring is poetry."

Suki uncrosses her legs and leans forward, elbows on knees, glass dangling from her fingers. The fluorescent light above the cutting table catches her glasses and turns them into two white shields. I can't see her eyes. It makes it easier to talk.
"Tell me about the design," she says. "The technical challenge. I want to understand what's got you working until one in the morning and not sleeping since Thursday."

So I tell her. I tell her about the shoulder construction — how Rowan's shoulders are unexpectedly level for a man who carries so much tension, how the right shoulder sits a millimeter higher than the left only when he's anxious, how I'm building an internal structure that accommodates both states. I tell her about the fabric — the charcoal wool with the faintest lavender undertone that only appears in certain light, how it shifts color the way water shifts color depending on the sky above it. I tell her about the lapels, the gorge line, the button stance, the way the draping falls over his frame as if the fabric was born knowing the shape of him.
I'm two fingers into the bourbon and a thousand words into the description when Suki holds up a hand.
"Orlando."
I stop.
"You're describing him like you're in love with him."
The hum spikes. A sharp, physical thing — like a hand squeezing my heart.
"I'm describing him like I'm designing for him," I say. "That's what good designers do. We study the body. We learn it. We—"
"Good designers don't look like they've been punched when they talk about shoulder seams."
I open my mouth. Close it.

Suki sets her glass down on the cutting table with a deliberate click. She doesn't say anything else. She doesn't have to. The silence does her work for her — fills the studio with the weight of a thing I can't admit, not to her, not to myself, not aloud.

Because if I say it — if I even let the thought fully form — I'll have to say the rest. I'll have to say: I saw his moleskine. I saw the sketches. My face in different genders, different configurations, the same bone structure repeated like a motif. He's dreaming of me. Not this me — not the me standing here right now in this body with these hands and this jaw — but the me that's coming. The me that the hum is building toward.
And if I say that, then I have to explain the hum.
And if I explain the hum, then I have to explain everything.
"I need the bathroom," I say.
Suki waves a hand. Go.
The lock clicks behind me and I lean against the door and breathe.

The bathroom is small — a toilet, a sink, a mirror, a fluorescent bar light that buzzes like an insect trapped in glass. I've been meaning to replace it. I won't. The buzz is almost a companion, almost a cover for the hum, though nothing really covers the hum. It just layers on top, two frequencies at war, and my body the instrument they're both playing.

I pull my shirt over my head. The air is cool against my skin and I feel the goosebumps rise, feel the hum ripple through them like wind through water. I stand in front of the mirror and I look at myself.
This body.

Female. Short hair — dark, almost black, cut close on the sides and longer on top, the way I've worn it for three years. Deep classical eyes, my mother called them — the kind that belong in a Renaissance painting, too large and too serious for a face this angular. A thin mouth. A jaw that I'm staring at now, tilting my head, trying to see.
Is it different?

I grip the edge of the sink and lean in. The fluorescent buzzes above me. My reflection stares back with those too-large eyes and I examine my own jaw — the line of it, the angle, the way it meets my ear. Is it broader? Is the curve slightly different than it was last month, last week, yesterday?
I can never tell in the early stages.

That's the cruelest part. The body doesn't announce itself. It doesn't pull a curtain and reveal a new actor on the stage. It betrays you millimeter by millimeter, cell by cell, a slow rewrite that you can't quite see until one morning you wake up and the face in the mirror belongs to a stranger. The jaw has widened. The brow has thickened. The hands are larger. The hips have shifted. And everyone around you acts like nothing has changed — or they don't act like nothing has changed, they flinch and stare and don't say anything, which is worse.

I look at my knuckles. Are they broader than last week? I stretch my fingers wide and watch the tendons move under the skin. I press my hands flat on the sink — the same way I pressed them against the floorboards — and I feel the hum there too, vibrating through the porcelain, through the pipes in the wall, through the century-old bones of this building.
Nine years.

This body — this specific configuration of bone and muscle and skin — has been mine for nine years. The longest stretch I've ever managed. I've been Orlando for nine years in this shape, and the shape has become so familiar that I'd almost forgotten it's temporary. Almost. The hum won't let me forget.

I lean forward until my forehead presses against the cold glass of the mirror. The cold is a shock — bright, clean, real — and I hold it there, feeling my breath fog the surface beneath my face, watching my own eyes go blurry through the condensation.
Not yet.
Please.

Not yet. Not now. Not while the suit is still on the dress form. Not while he's looking at me with those dark, knowing eyes. Not while his moleskine is full of my face in forms I haven't taken yet. If I shift now — if I become someone else — then the woman he's been designing for will disappear, and what takes her place might not fit the pattern he's been sketching. Might not fit the fabric. Might not fit his hands when he reaches for me in the workshop and adjusts the shoulder seam with his fingertips on my collarbone.
I'm not ready. That's the lie I tell the mirror. I'm not ready.

The truth is simpler and worse: I can't stop it. The hum is a frequency, and it's been called out of me — by proximity, by attention, by being seen. By him. Every day I spend in his orbit is a day the pitch gets higher, the vibration gets deeper, the countdown ticks closer to zero. And I can't stay away.
The glass is slick with my breath. I pull my forehead away and a cold, wet kiss of condensation marks where I was.
A knock. Three beats. The heartbeat stutter.
"Orlando? You okay in there?"
Suki's voice through the door. Low. No judgment. Just the question, held out like a hand.

I pull my shirt back on. The fabric catches on my damp forehead and I tug it down, run my fingers through my hair, look at myself one more time. The jaw. The knuckles. The eyes that are too large and too serious for this face. Still mine. Still this version. Still here.
I unlock the door and open it.

Suki is standing in the hallway with the bottle in one hand and my glass — refilled — in the other. She takes one look at my face and I see her catalog everything. The fear. The exhaustion. The red mark on my forehead from the glass. She sees all of it and she doesn't ask a single question because Suki Tanaka learned a long time ago that asking me questions I can't answer just makes me retreat further, and she'd rather have me in the room than right.
She holds out the glass.
"Whatever this is," she says, "you don't have to carry it alone. Stop acting like you've never had a friend before."

I take the glass. I almost laugh — a real laugh, the kind that breaks open something in your chest and lets the air in. Almost. What stops me isn't the fear. It's the knowledge that if I laugh, I might start talking, and if I start talking, I might tell her, and if I tell her, everything changes.

So I drink instead. The bourbon burns at last — proper burn, the kind that lights you up from the inside and reminds you that you're still here, still flesh, still a body bound by time and chemistry and the slow inevitability of change.
Suki puts her hand on my shoulder. Brief. Firm. A point of contact in the dark.
The hum continues.

It's louder now than it was an hour ago. It's louder than it was yesterday. Louder than it was last week when I first stepped into Rowan Ashworth's orbit and felt the frequency spike like a seismograph reading an earthquake that hasn't happened yet.
Nine years of silence. Nine years of stillness. And the hum is building, building, building — a bass note in my bones that I can't tune out, can't drown out, can't outrun.
Every day I stay near him is a day closer to the shift.
And I can't stay away.
I can't stay away.
